title: ROUGE: Evaluation Metrics
concept: ROUGE-L and ROUGE-2 check n-gram overlap between model summaries and human references to measure factual coverage.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary use of the ROUGE metric in machine learning?

options.text: Translating audio files to different languages

exercises.explain: ROUGE stands for Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation. It is widely used to evaluate automatic summarization systems.

cards.title: Grading the AI's Homework
cards.body:
Imagine you write a summary of a movie, and your friend does too. How do we test if a computer's summary is just as good? 

Instead of guessing, we use ROUGE to count the exact words and phrases they share. It's like a spot-the-difference game for summaries!

cards.title: What is ROUGE?
cards.body:
ROUGE stands for Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation. It compares an AI-generated summary against a human-written "reference" summary. 

It specifically measures how much of the human summary's key information was successfully captured by the AI.

cards.title: The Landscape Grid
cards.body:
Imagine you painted a landscape, and your teacher has a picture of the same spot. 

Instead of looking at the artistic style, your teacher places a grid over both pictures and counts how many leaves, trees, and clouds are in the exact same positions. ROUGE does this with words to see how much of the original scenery your summary captured!

cards.title: Spotting the Overlap
cards.body:
Let's say the human reference summary is:
"The brown fox jumped."

If the AI writes: "A brown fox jumped.", ROUGE-2 looks for matching pairs of words (2-grams):
• "brown fox" (Match!)
• "fox jumped" (Match!)

This high overlap gives the AI summary a great ROUGE score!

cards.title: Does High Score Mean Perfect Writing?
cards.body:
People think a perfect ROUGE score means a summary is beautifully written. 

Actually, ROUGE only counts matching words! A summary could have a high ROUGE score but still sound robotic or repeat the same sentences. It measures coverage, not creativity!

cards.title: Visualizing ROUGE Types
cards.body: ROUGE measures how much of the human reference summary was captured by the AI summary using different word grouping methods.

right.text: Looks at the longest sequence of matching words in order, even if they have other words in between.

left.text: Looks at matching pairs of consecutive words (e.g., "brown fox", "fox jumped") to check local phrasing.

exercises.prompt: What does "recall" in the context of ROUGE measure?

options.text: How much information from the human reference was captured by the AI summary.
The speed at which the AI generated the summary.
The number of spelling mistakes in the final output.
Whether the summary uses fancy vocabulary.

exercises.explain: ROUGE calculates recall by checking how many words or phrases from the human reference summary actually made it into the AI's summary.

exercises.prompt: True or False: ROUGE-L evaluates summaries by finding the longest common sequence of words between the AI and human texts.
exercises.explain: ROUGE-L looks for the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) to find the longest sequence of words that appear in the same relative order in both texts.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to specify the metric that looks at pairs of consecutive words.
exercises.sentence: To check if pairs of words match the reference summary, we use the [blank] metric.
exercises.explain: ROUGE-2 measures the overlap of two-word sequences (bigrams), which helps track if phrase meanings are preserved.

exercises.prompt: If the human reference summary is: "A quick brown fox jumped over the lazy dog." Which AI output would get the highest ROUGE score?

options.text: "The quick brown jumped over lazily dog."
"A quick yellow cat slept quietly on the rug."
"The quick brown fox jumped over the lazy dog."

exercises.explain: The first option contains a grammatical error and doesn't make logical sense. The third option uses correct, natural language that shares maximum overlap with the reference.

exercises.prompt: How do ROUGE-2 and ROUGE-L differ in how they evaluate a summary?

options.text: ROUGE-2 checks the length, while ROUGE-L checks the grammar.
ROUGE-2 checks pairs of words, while ROUGE-L checks the longest shared word sequence.
ROUGE-2 ignores word order, while ROUGE-L forces words to be side-by-side.
ROUGE-2 counts single words, while ROUGE-L counts paragraph spacing.

exercises.explain: ROUGE-L looks at the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) to check sentence structure, while ROUGE-2 looks at exact pairs of words (2-grams) in order. Together, they balance flow and exact vocabulary matching!
